Germany.title: Germany
gridConnectionProcedure: Renewable energy plants have a statutory right to prompt grid connection under the Renewable Energy Sources Act, especially Section 8 EEG.

Grid operators are required to connect renewable installations at the technically and economically suitable grid connection point. For low-voltage installations, the practical connection process is shaped by the Low Voltage Connection Ordinance, the technical connection rules and the requirements of the local distribution system operator.

Distributed generation units connected to the low-voltage grid must comply with VDE-AR-N 4105, which includes requirements for dynamic grid support, reactive power provision and stable behaviour during voltage disturbances.
strategiesvolatility: Germany has introduced a highly relevant mechanism through Section 14a EnWG, allowing distribution system operators to temporarily reduce the power of controllable consumption devices during imminent grid congestion
The mechanism applies particularly to heat pumps, private EV charging points, battery storage systems and air-conditioning units. In return, consumers receive reduced network charges
Grid-supporting inverter behaviour under VDE-AR-N 4105 helps to stabilise voltage in low-voltage networks with high photovoltaic penetration
regulatoryFramework: Energy Industry Act: EnWG, especially Section 14a
Renewable Energy Sources Act: EEG, especially Sections 8 ff
Low Voltage Connection Ordinance: NAV
Metering Point Operation Act: MsbG
Technical rules: VDE-AR-N 4100 and VDE-AR-N 4105
responsibleInstitutions: Bundesnetzagentur: national regulatory authority
Local distribution system operators: e.g. Westnetz, Bayernwerk, Netze BW, E.DIS
VDE FNN: technical connection rules
implementationExamples: From 2024 onwards, new controllable devices cannot generally be refused connection merely because of potential congestion; instead, the DSO may temporarily limit consumption during critical grid situations.
EV chargers or heat pumps may be reduced to a lower operating level rather than disconnected completely.
Reduced network charges create a financial incentive for consumers to provide flexibility.
newconsumersandflexibleloads: Electric vehicles, heat pumps, battery storage systems and air-conditioning units are explicitly treated as controllable consumption devices under the new Section 14a framework

sources.name: Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy (BMWE)

comparison.gridOperators: TSOs: 50Hertz, Amprion, TenneT, TransnetBW; DSOs: local network operators
comparison.connectionProcedure: Application to DSO → technical assessment → compliance with TAB/VDE rules → metering/control equipment → grid connection
comparison.legalBasis: EnWG, EEG, NAV, MsbG, §14a EnWG, VDE-AR-N 4105
comparison.implementationExample: Controllable EV chargers, heat pumps and batteries may be temporarily limited during grid congestion in exchange for reduced grid fees

Austria.title: Austria
Austria.gridConnectionProcedure: Renewable energy installations are connected to the grid in Austria through the responsible grid operators. The rapid expansion of small and medium-sized PV systems, in particular, is creating new requirements in low-voltage networks: feed-in peaks, voltage increases, bidirectional power flows, and a growing need for grid reinforcement. E-Control has therefore highlighted measures to accelerate grid connections and ensure the transparent handling of connection requests. For the project, Austria is particularly interesting because grid connection is being further developed not only technically but also organizationally and regulatorily.
strategiesvolatility: Austria combines several flexibility options: hydropower and pumped-storage plants, smart meters, battery storage systems, smart grids, demand-side management, and energy communities. Smart Grids Austria and APG particularly emphasize the importance of intelligent grids for integrating renewable energy. At the distribution network level, intelligent local substations, grid monitoring, and controllable consumers are important technical solutions
regulatoryFramework: Key legal foundations include the Renewable Expansion Act (EAG), the Electricity Industry and Organization Act 2010 (ElWOG 2010), EU Directive 2018/2001 on renewable energy, and EU Directive 2019/944 on the internal electricity market. The EAG is particularly important because it provides the legal basis for energy communities and support mechanisms. ElWOG regulates market organization, grid operation, market roles, and security of supply.
responsibleInstitutions: E-Control is the national regulatory authority supervising the electricity and gas markets. The Federal Ministry for Climate Action (BMK) is responsible for energy policy. Austrian Power Grid (APG) is the transmission system operator and system manager. Distribution system operators are responsible for local grid connections. The Austrian Coordination Office for Energy Communities supports practical implementation.
implementationExamples: Energy communities play a particularly important role in Austria. The Renewable Expansion Act (EAG) enables Renewable Energy Communities and Citizen Energy Communities. This allows citizens, municipalities, small and medium-sized enterprises, and public institutions to jointly generate, consume, store, and sell electricity. The primary benefit is to use locally generated PV electricity as locally as possible. This can reduce grid losses, increase public acceptance of renewable energy, and create new business models for neighborhoods, municipalities, and regional cooperations.
newconsumersandflexibleloads: New consumers mainly include heat pumps, EV charging stations (wallboxes), battery storage systems, and prosumer households. These increase the local grid load but can also support the grid through intelligent control. Heat pumps can use thermal storage, electric vehicles can shift charging times, and battery storage systems can reduce PV feed-in peaks. As a result, flexibility in distribution grids has become a key component of Austria’s energy transition.

sources.name: Renewable Expansion Act (EAG), Republic of Austria / RIS (2021)

comparison.gridOperators: Austrian Power Grid (APG); regional DSOs (e.g. Netz NÖ, Netz OÖ, Wiener Netze)
comparison.connectionProcedure: Connection request → grid capacity assessment → technical approval → installation according to TOR → commissioning
comparison.legalBasis: Elektrizitätswirtschafts- und -organisationsgesetz (ElWOG), Erneuerbaren-Ausbau-Gesetz (EAG), TOR Technical Rules
comparison.implementationExample: Smart Grids Austria projects integrate PV, heat pumps and e-mobility through digital distribution networks and flexibility management.

Turkey.title: Turkey
gridConnectionProcedure: The process comprises six steps: proof of an active electricity subscription, technical application to the regional distribution system operator (EDAŞ), a 30-day evaluation by the energy authority (EİGM), physical installation, smart meter installation, and finally the transition to grid-connected operation.
strategiesvolatility: Turkey statically caps the installable PV capacity at twice the contracted connection capacity
Furthermore, to dampen midday peaks, monthly net metering was abolished and replaced by an hourly settlement to force prosumers into simultaneous self-consumption
regulatoryFramework: The central regulatory framework is the Regulation on Unlicensed Electricity Generation (Lisanssız Elektrik Üretimi)
For large-scale projects, a mandatory coupling applies where grid connection capacities are only granted in a 1:1 ratio with the installation of battery storage systems
responsibleInstitutions: The regulatory authority is the EPDK
The transmission grid is centrally operated by TEİAŞ
The distribution grid is divided into 21 regional monopoly areas (EDAŞ)
implementationExamples: To avoid prolonged load peaks and blockages at grid nodes, an automated curtailment applies to DC fast-charging stations: charging processes can be hard-disconnected by the grid once an 85% state of charge is reached
newconsumersandflexibleloads: Battery storage is voluntarily permitted for private households
To prevent grey electricity arbitrage, electricity fed back into the grid from storage systems is unremunerated and flows into the state YEKDEM funding pool
EV charging infrastructure operators require a direct license from the EPDK

sources.name: ACER (Agency for the Cooperation of Energy Regulators)

comparison.gridOperators: TEİAŞ; regional distribution companies (e.g. Başkent EDAŞ, AYEDAŞ, Toroslar EDAŞ)
comparison.connectionProcedure: Connection application → capacity evaluation → technical compliance → connection agreement → commissioning
comparison.legalBasis: Electricity Market Law No. 6446, Electricity Market Connection and System Use Regulation, Distributed Generation Regulation
comparison.implementationExample: Rapid expansion of rooftop PV and distributed generation supported by simplified licensing and connection procedures for renewable installations

Portugal.title: Portugal
gridConnectionProcedure: Portugal’s electricity system is governed by Decree-Law No. 15/2022, which establishes the organisation and functioning of the national electricity system, the Sistema Elétrico Nacional.
Self-consumption installations, known as UPAC, are registered and operated under the Portuguese self-consumption framework.
Connection to the public electricity grid, RESP, involves technical verification by the relevant network operator and regulatory compliance with ERSE rules.
strategiesvolatility: Portugal strongly relies on self-consumption, collective self-consumption and renewable energy communities to balance generation and demand closer to the point of use.
The self-consumption regulation defines 15-minute energy balancing intervals, which is relevant for allocating consumption and injection in decentralised systems.
Smart grid services and digital metering are used to improve data availability, transparency and network operation.
regulatoryFramework: Decree-Law No. 15/2022: general electricity system law.
ERSE Regulation No. 2/2023: self-consumption regulation.
ERSE rules on smart grids, metering and electric mobility.
responsibleInstitutions: ERSE: national regulatory authority for energy services.
DGEG: Directorate-General for Energy and Geology, responsible for licensing and administrative energy procedures.
E-REDES: main distribution system operator.
implementationExamples: Collective self-consumption allows several consumers to share locally produced renewable electricity.
Renewable energy communities can use UPAC generation and distribute benefits among members.
E-REDES provides operational information for production licensing and connection to the RESP.
newconsumersandflexibleloads: UPAC installations, battery storage, renewable energy communities, EV charging infrastructure and collective self-consumption schemes.
Electric mobility is regulated through ERSE’s Electric Mobility Regulation, covering charging point operators, service providers, measurement, data provision and price supervision.

comparison.connectionProcedure: Registration/licensing → technical verification → connection to RESP → metering and settlement
comparison.legalBasis: Decree-Law No. 15/2022, ERSE Regulation No. 2/2023 (Self-Consumption), Electric Mobility Regulation
comparison.implementationExample: Collective self-consumption (UPAC) and Renewable Energy Communities (CER) share locally generated renewable electricity.

France.title: France
gridConnectionProcedure: The process runs digitally via the Enedis Connect portal. After the application is submitted, a connection offer is provided within 6 weeks. Before commissioning and activating the Linky smart meter, a safety certificate from the independent authority CONSUEL must be presented.
strategiesvolatility: France uses a nationally standardized tariff signal control system (Heures Pleines / Heures Creuses or Tempo tariff)
The smart meters receive price signals and fully automatically activate or block large thermal consumers depending on grid utilization
regulatoryFramework: The system is based on surplus feed-in with state-guaranteed purchase tariffs (Obligation d'achat) by EDF
Pure net metering does not exist
responsibleInstitutions: The Commission de Régulation de l'Énergie (CRE) acts as the NRA
RTE controls the transmission grid, while Enedis holds a de facto monopoly in the distribution grid with a market share of around 95%
implementationExamples: The concept of Autoconsommation collective allows several households within a 2-kilometer radius in the low-voltage grid to balance their generation and consumption data hourly without having to lay direct physical cables.
newconsumersandflexibleloads: Battery storage plays a minor role in the residential sector due to stable feed-in tariffs
Private wallboxes must be controllable by the grid ex-works
Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) technologies are being introduced commercially on a broad scale

Spain.title: Spain
Spain.gridConnectionProcedure: The connection of renewable energy installations is shaped by national and regional approval procedures, technical connection requirements, and grid capacity assessments. Large PV and wind projects are often connected to Red Eléctrica's transmission network, while self-consumption systems and community PV projects are mainly connected to distribution grids. The key challenge is balancing very high solar generation peaks with regional demand, available grid capacity, and storage.
strategiesvolatility: Spain relies on grid expansion, energy storage, self-consumption, collective self-consumption, flexibility, and improved market integration of renewable energy. PV self-consumption can reduce electricity fed into the public grid when generation and consumption coincide locally. Battery storage and flexible loads are becoming increasingly important for absorbing midday PV peaks and shifting demand into the evening.
regulatoryFramework: The main legal foundations are Royal Decree 244/2019 on self-consumption, Royal Decree-Law 23/2020, and other implementations of European rules concerning Renewable Energy Communities and Citizen Energy Communities. The EU Renewable Energy Directive and the EU Electricity Market Directive are also relevant. Regulatory priorities focus on simplifying self-consumption, collective use, metering, billing, and grid access.
responsibleInstitutions: MITECO is the ministry responsible for ecological transition and energy policy. IDAE supports programs, advisory services, and implementation of self-consumption and energy communities. CNMC is the national regulatory authority for competition and markets. Red Eléctrica (REE) is the transmission system operator and system manager. Regional authorities and distribution system operators play a key role in approval and grid connection processes.
implementationExamples: Spain’s best practice lies in expanding PV self-consumption, collective self-consumption, and local energy communities. For Germany, neighborhood models, shared PV use, storage integration, and simplified billing models are particularly relevant. Spain demonstrates that regulatory simplification is a key success factor for prosumers, apartment buildings, and municipal energy communities.
newconsumersandflexibleloads: New consumers in Spain primarily include electric vehicles, heat pumps in certain building segments, battery storage systems, prosumers, and commercial self-consumption systems. Collective self-consumption is especially important: multiple consumers can jointly use a single generation facility, for example in apartment buildings, neighborhoods, or municipal projects. This transforms consumers into active market participants.

comparison.connectionProcedure: Connection application → network study → compliance with CEI standards → metering → grid connection
comparison.legalBasis: Legislative Decree 199/2021, ARERA resolutions, CEI 0-21 (LV), CEI 0-16 (MV)
comparison.implementationExample: Nationwide deployment of second-generation smart meters (Open Meter) and extensive PV integration into low-voltage networks

Netherlands.title: Netherlands
gridConnectionProcedure: The Dutch electricity system is regulated through the Netcode Elektriciteit, which sets rules for network operation, grid connection and electricity transport.
Due to severe grid congestion, grid connection is no longer only a technical procedure but increasingly a capacity allocation problem.
In congested areas, new renewable generation, batteries, heat pumps, charging stations and industrial loads may face waiting lists or conditional connection arrangements.
strategiesvolatility: The Netherlands is one of Europe’s most important case studies for grid congestion management. ACM has introduced measures to incentivise large users to reduce or avoid grid use during peak hours.
Congestion management rules allow system operators and large users to free up capacity for renewable projects, EV charging infrastructure, heat pumps and other electrified loads.
Flexible grid use, off-peak contracts and congestion steering contracts are increasingly used to make better use of existing capacity.
regulatoryFramework: Electricity Act
Netcode Elektriciteit
ACM code decisions on congestion management and flexible use of the grid
responsibleInstitutions: ACM: Netherlands Authority for Consumers and Markets, national regulatory authority.
TenneT: transmission system operator.
Regional DSOs: Liander, Enexis, Stedin.
implementationExamples: ACM has approved measures that financially reward large users for reducing consumption during peak periods.
TenneT has made more than 9 GW of capacity available through off-peak flexible contracts.
The Netherlands is therefore a strong reference country for flexibility before reinforcement.
newconsumersandflexibleloads: EV charging stations, heat pumps, electric boilers, battery systems, data centres and industrial electrification projects.
These loads are relevant because they intensify peak demand and contribute to congestion in both transmission and distribution grids.

sources.name: Ministry of Climate Policy and Green Growth

Denmark.title: Denmark
gridConnectionProcedure: Danish grid connection is coordinated through Energinet, distribution companies and technical rules for production and demand installations. Energinet publishes rules, conditions and methods for grid connection and system operation.
Renewable generators must comply with national requirements and European network codes, including requirements for wind turbines, photovoltaic plants and other renewable technologies.
Low-voltage demand installations are covered by technical guidance for connection to the low-voltage grid.
strategiesvolatility: Denmark uses market-based flexibility, dynamic price signals and short-term electricity markets to integrate large shares of wind and solar power.
Energinet highlights that the need for flexibility increases with renewable energy expansion and that 15-minute trading supports better balancing of forecast errors and variable generation.
Danish regulators emphasise cost-reflective tariffs, transparency and non-discrimination when designing flexibility-oriented tariff structures.
regulatoryFramework: Danish Electricity Supply Act
Danish Renewable Energy Act
Executive orders on grid connection of wind turbines, photovoltaic power stations, wave farms and hydropower plants
Energinet technical rules and market rules
responsibleInstitutions: Danish Utility Regulator: national regulatory authority
Energinet: transmission system operator
Distribution system operators such as N1 and Radius/Andel
implementationExamples: 15-minute intraday trading and balancing market reforms allow market participants to adjust production and consumption closer to real time
Tariff reform and digitalisation are being used to activate demand-side flexibility
newconsumersandflexibleloads: Electric vehicles, heat pumps, data centres, Power-to-X, electrolysers and industrial electrification are considered important future flexibility resources
Denmark is especially relevant because demand-side flexibility is linked to wind power integration and sector coupling

sources.name: Energinet – Rules, Regulations, Conditions and Methods
Guide for Grid Connection of Demand Installations to the Low Voltage Grid
Renewable Energy Act (Lov om fremme af vedvarende energi)

Norway.title: Norway
gridConnectionProcedure: Norway applies grid connection codes for generators, demand connections and HVDC systems as part of Norwegian energy regulation
The Norwegian energy system is regulated by NVE/RME, with the Energy Act forming the main legal framework for electricity market regulation
Norway is not an EU Member State, but it is closely integrated into the Nordic and European electricity system through EEA-related regulation and Nordic market cooperation
strategiesvolatility: Norway is highly relevant because of its very high EV penetration and strong reliance on electric heating
Capacity-based grid tariffs for low-voltage customers were introduced to incentivise consumers to reduce peak loads and shift flexible consumption
Non-firm connection agreements allow grid companies to curtail consumption under agreed conditions, reducing the need for immediate grid reinforcement
regulatoryFramework: Norwegian Energy Act
NVE/RME regulations on grid tariffs
Norwegian implementation of grid connection codes: RfG, DCC and HVDC
responsibleInstitutions: NVE: Norwegian Water Resources and Energy Directorate
RME: Norwegian Energy Regulatory Authority
Statnett: transmission system operator
Regional distribution system operators
implementationExamples: Capacity-based network tariffs encourage households to avoid simultaneous high-power consumption
Smart EV charging is used as a practical instrument to reduce local peak loads
Non-firm connection agreements provide a regulatory tool for connecting flexible demand without immediately expanding the grid
newconsumersandflexibleloads: Electric vehicles, electric heating, heat pumps, household batteries, distributed solar PV and flexible industrial demand
EV charging is particularly important because unmanaged charging can create local peak-load problems, while smart charging can defer or reduce network reinforcement needs

sources.name: Norwegian Water Resources and Energy Directorate (NVE)

Finland.title: Finland
gridConnectionProcedure: The registration depends on the system capacity. Under 16 amps per phase (Form G98), the Fit & Inform principle applies (installation and subsequent notification within 28 days). Larger systems (G99) require a 45-day prior assessment by the grid operator
strategiesvolatility: The introduction of Market-wide Half-Hourly Settlement (MHHS) forces the entire market into a strict 30-minute billing grid, which incentivizes end customers to significantly shift their load via highly dynamic tariffs
regulatoryFramework: The Smart Export Guarantee (SEG) system obliges large suppliers to offer feed-in tariffs
These tariffs are not fixed by the state but are subject to free market competition
responsibleInstitutions: The market is regulated by Ofgem
The transmission grid is managed by the National Energy System Operator (NESO)
The distribution grid is divided into 14 regions controlled by 6 large DNO (Distribution Network Operator) conglomerates
implementationExamples: The Electric Vehicles Smart Charge Points Regulations mandate by law that new private wallboxes must be configured by default to block charging processes during peak grid hours (8-11 AM and 4-7 PM)
newconsumersandflexibleloads: The UK is a leader in local flexibility markets
DNOs regularly tender Flexibility Services, where aggregators bundle the capacity of private storage systems and heat pumps and use them for local grid stabilization in exchange for remuneration

United Kingdom.title: United Kingdom
gridConnectionProcedure: The registration depends on the system capacity. Under 16 amps per phase (Form G98), the Fit & Inform principle applies (installation and subsequent notification within 28 days). Larger systems (G99) require a 45-day prior assessment by the grid operator.
strategiesvolatility: The introduction of Market-wide Half-Hourly Settlement (MHHS) forces the entire market into a strict 30-minute billing grid, which incentivizes end customers to significantly shift their load via highly dynamic tariffs
regulatoryFramework: The Smart Export Guarantee (SEG) system obliges large suppliers to offer feed-in tariffs
These tariffs are not fixed by the state but are subject to free market competition
responsibleInstitutions: The market is regulated by Ofgem
The transmission grid is managed by the National Energy System Operator (NESO)
The distribution grid is divided into 14 regions controlled by 6 large DNO (Distribution Network Operator) conglomerates
implementationExamples: The Electric Vehicles Smart Charge Points Regulations mandate by law that new private wallboxes must be configured by default to block charging processes during peak grid hours (8-11 AM and 4-7 PM)
newconsumersandflexibleloads: The UK is a leader in local flexibility markets
DNOs regularly tender Flexibility Services, where aggregators bundle the capacity of private storage systems and heat pumps and use them for local grid stabilization in exchange for remuneration

sources.name: Energy Networks Association (ENA) Guidelines G98/ G99
UK Department for Energy Security and Net Zero

Belgium.title: Belgium (Flanders)
Belgium.gridConnectionProcedure: The process strictly requires hardware conformity with the Belgian Synergrid list (C10/11). After installation, a physical safety inspection (AREI) by a control body must take place. Upon approval, a digital meter is installed as a priority
strategiesvolatility: Flanders has completely abolished net metering and primarily combats volatility via grid fees
Instead of billed kilowatt-hours, a large part of the grid costs is calculated based on the highest measured 15-minute power peak (kW) of the month
regulatoryFramework: The regulatory environment is highly fragmented and dictated regionally
While Wallonia and Brussels partly still rely on classic systems, Flanders forces extreme local load smoothing through the pure surplus model
responsibleInstitutions: Alongside the federal CREG
the regionals VREG (Flanders)
CWaPE (Wallonia)
BRUGEL (Brussels)
In Flanders, the large corporation Fluvius dominates the distribution grid as a monopolist
implementationExamples: Intelligent Home Energy Management Systems (HEMS) actively throttle the wallbox to a minimum in Flanders as soon as the heat pump or stove is activated in the household, so that the power peak does not rise above the minimum capacity tariff threshold of 2.5 kW
newconsumersandflexibleloads: Battery storage systems record extremely high installation rates due to the capacity tariff, as they function as the primary peak-shaving unit in the home

sources.name: Belgian Federal Commission for Electricity and Gas Regulation (CREG)
Brussels regulatory authority for electricity, gas and water price control
